fact: December 8 is exactly 23 days before New Year's Eve, which means if you started a countdown today, you'd say 'twenty-three!' really loud!
In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun sets so early on December 8 that some places get dark before 4:30 PM - that's earlier than most kids finish their after-school snacks!
Reindeer in the Arctic are typically shedding their summer antler velvet around this time, leaving behind shiny bone antlers that look like nature's own crowns.
December 8 falls in the middle of Sagittarius season for those who follow zodiac signs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, December 8 is prime summer prep time - jacaranda trees often bloom with bright purple flowers that carpet the ground like nature's confetti.
The winter solstice is only 13 days away from December 8, making this one of the darkest times of year in places like Alaska, where some towns get less than 4 hours of daylight.
Gray whales are usually migrating south along the Pacific coast around December 8, traveling up to 100 miles per day - that's like swimming from one city to another every single day!
In many cold regions, December 8 typically marks the time when pond ice becomes thick enough for the first brave ducks to start walking on it instead of swimming.
The Geminid meteor shower is ramping up around December 8, which means you might see 'shooting stars' that are actually tiny space rocks burning up in our atmosphere at 130,000 miles per hour.
December 8 is day 342 of the year, leaving exactly 23 days left - the same number as the degrees Earth is tilted, which is why we have seasons in the first place!
In northern forests, evergreen trees like pine and spruce often produce their stickiest, smelliest sap around December 8 to protect themselves from freezing temperatures.
Arctic foxes are at their fluffiest around December 8, with winter coats so thick they can sleep comfortably on snow even when it's 40 degrees below zero!
